full_prompt: Is the use of predictive modeling justified when the training or validation data may be too narrow to represent the diversity of real material compositions and processing histories?
chosen:
I will anchor on a widely used materials-ML benchmarking paper because the question turns on whether reported predictive accuracy survives beyond narrow train/validation distributions. The anchor is Matbench, which formalizes standardized prediction tasks but also exposes the risk that benchmark splits may not represent real deployment diversity.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W3108737797"}
```

[result]: Read **Dunn et al., “Benchmarking materials property prediction methods: the Matbench test set and Automatminer reference algorithm,” npj Computational Materials, 2020**. The paper defines 13 benchmark datasets for materials property prediction and evaluates models under standardized train/test splits. Its contribution is methodological comparability, but its default validation regime is primarily benchmark-centered rather than explicitly designed around out-of-distribution composition, synthesis, or processing-history shifts.

Next I extract the “genome” of the anchor paper so the critical analysis can distinguish what the paper actually claims from what practitioners might over-infer from it. This is important because the user’s question asks about justification, not merely performance.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W3108737797"}
```

[result]: Extracted genome: **mechanism** = supervised ML maps composition/structure descriptors to materials properties; **niche** = standardized property-prediction benchmarks for materials informatics; **observation** = reference models achieve competitive error on curated datasets; **limitation** = benchmark accuracy may depend on dataset construction and split strategy; **delta** = standardizes evaluation across tasks; **claim** = Matbench enables reproducible comparison of materials-property predictors. The genome shows that the anchor justifies model comparison, not necessarily real-world deployment across arbitrary compositions and processing histories.

I now run a novelty check on the anchor mechanism to see whether the critical issue is novelty of prediction itself or the validation regime. The likely gap is not “can ML predict materials properties?” but “when is the validation population broad enough to support deployment claims?”

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"supervised materials-property prediction benchmarked on curated train/test splits using composition or structure features","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```

[result]: The novelty check finds many 2018–2025 works using CGCNN, MEGNet, CrabNet, Roost, Automatminer, and Matbench-style leaderboards. The recurring novelty is model architecture or benchmark performance, while fewer papers explicitly validate under chemical-family holdout, temporal holdout, laboratory/source holdout, or processing-route shift. This suggests the critical gap is external validity, not raw predictive modeling.

I search directly for limitations and critiques concerning narrow validation data, extrapolation, and out-of-distribution materials discovery. The goal is to identify empirical evidence that random validation can overstate model reliability.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"materials informatics machine learning extrapolation random split validation narrow composition space limitations","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: Search returned several relevant works: **https://openalex.org/W2806182414**, “Can machine learning identify the next high-temperature superconductor? Examining extrapolation performance for materials discovery,” npj Computational Materials, 2018; **https://openalex.org/W4388372789**, “Matbench Discovery: A benchmark for identifying stable crystals from first principles,” 2023/2024; **https://openalex.org/W3046889446**, “Roost: A machine learning approach for predicting inorganic materials properties without crystal structure,” 2020; and reviews on uncertainty and extrapolation in materials informatics. The strongest critique papers emphasize that IID/random splits can produce optimistic estimates when deployment requires extrapolation to new chemistries or discovery-time candidates.

I read the superconductivity extrapolation study because it directly tests whether ML trained on historically narrow data can discover materially novel examples. This is a close analogue to the user’s concern about unrepresented compositions.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W2806182414"}
```

[result]: Read **“Can machine learning identify the next high-temperature superconductor? Examining extrapolation performance for materials discovery,” npj Computational Materials, 2018**. The study shows that models can score well under conventional validation yet struggle when asked to extrapolate to historically later or chemically distinct superconductors. Its key lesson is that retrospective random validation can be insufficient evidence for discovery claims when the deployment target lies outside the training distribution.

I next read a more recent benchmark designed around discovery-style validation, because it offers a possible fix rather than only a critique. This helps move from “predictive modeling may be unjustified” to “predictive modeling is conditionally justified under stronger validation.”

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W4388372789"}
```

[result]: Read **“Matbench Discovery: A benchmark for identifying stable crystals from first principles,” 2023/2024**. The paper shifts emphasis from random property-prediction accuracy to prospective/discovery-relevant evaluation, including identifying stable materials among candidates not trivially represented in training. It supports the idea that materials ML should be evaluated against deployment-like candidate pools, with attention to novelty, stability, and distribution shift.

The anchor and critique papers point to a fixed-method validation experiment: keep predictive modeling, but stress-test it against composition and processing-history diversity rather than relying on narrow random splits. I now propose the validation study.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Train supervised materials-property models on composition, structure, and, where available, processing-history descriptors; evaluate them under multiple split regimes: IID random split, composition-family holdout, processing-route holdout, source/laboratory holdout, temporal holdout, and combined out-of-distribution holdout. Add uncertainty calibration and applicability-domain estimation so predictions outside represented regions are flagged rather than treated as equally reliable.","niche_genome":"Materials informatics for property prediction and materials discovery, especially cases where datasets are curated from limited composition classes, synthesis routes, laboratories, databases, or processing histories. The experiment targets deployment settings where the candidate materials differ from the training data in chemistry, microstructure, or processing route.","observation_genome":"Prior benchmarks such as Matbench demonstrate reproducible model comparison under standardized splits, while extrapolation-focused studies show that high random-split accuracy can fail to translate to discovery of chemically or temporally novel materials. Discovery-oriented benchmarks indicate that validation should mimic the intended deployment population rather than only the sampled historical dataset.","limitation_genome":"If training or validation data are too narrow, conventional predictive modeling is not fully justified for broad deployment claims. Narrow data can hide blind spots, inflate accuracy through similarity between train and test examples, and omit processing-induced variation that strongly affects properties. Even uncertainty estimates may be miscalibrated if the validation set does not include realistic out-of-domain cases.","delta_genome":"Replace single random-split validation with a coverage-stress protocol: quantify dataset coverage over composition, structure, processing, and source; report performance decay from IID to OOD splits; require calibrated uncertainty on held-out families/routes; and define a pre-registered deployment domain. Models are considered justified only inside domains where both error and uncertainty remain acceptable under the relevant holdout test.","claim_genome":"Predictive modeling is justified when used as interpolation within a validated applicability domain, or as a triage tool with explicit uncertainty and OOD safeguards. It is not justified as a general claim over real materials diversity when the training and validation data are too narrow to represent composition and processing-history variation. The proposed validation experiment converts the question from a yes/no judgment into an empirical criterion: if performance and calibration survive composition/process/source/temporal holdouts, deployment is defensible; if they collapse, the model should be restricted or the dataset expanded."}}
```

[result]: Proposed validation: a **materials-ML external-validity stress test** that compares IID benchmark accuracy against composition-, processing-, source-, and time-based holdouts. The expected outcome is not that predictive modeling should be abandoned, but that its claims must be scoped to the data’s demonstrated coverage. This directly addresses whether narrow training/validation data justify real-world materials predictions.

full_prompt: Compare Kantian and virtue ethical approaches to truth-telling. Are there circumstances in which lying is morally justified?
chosen:
A direct proposal is sufficient: the topic is a standard ethics comparison, and the genome can frame a concise argumentative answer.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Compare Kantian deontology, which treats truth-telling as a perfect duty grounded in respect for rational agency, with virtue ethics, which evaluates truthfulness through practical wisdom, character, and context-sensitive flourishing.", "niche_genome": "Applied normative ethics question about whether lying can ever be morally justified, especially in cases involving harm prevention, protection of the innocent, or conflicts among moral goods.", "observation_genome": "Kantian ethics generally forbids lying because deception undermines autonomy and cannot be universalized without contradiction; virtue ethics values honesty as a virtue but allows that truthful action must be guided by phronesis and balanced against compassion, justice, and courage.", "limitation_genome": "A strict Kantian view can seem morally rigid in cases such as lying to a murderer, while virtue ethics may risk vagueness because it lacks a simple rule for when deception becomes justified.", "delta_genome": "The key contrast is that Kantian ethics asks whether lying violates a universal moral duty, whereas virtue ethics asks whether lying in a specific situation expresses the character and judgment of a practically wise person.", "claim_genome": "On a Kantian approach, lying is rarely or never morally justified; on a virtue ethical approach, lying may be justified in exceptional circumstances when it protects important human goods and reflects practical wisdom rather than cowardice, selfishness, or manipulation."}}
```

{"tool":"propose","answer":"建议一家三口选择广州直飞新加坡，5天4晚控制在25000元以内：机票按三人往返约6000–9000元估算，酒店建议住武吉士 Bugis、市政厅 City Hall 或乌节路 Orchard 一带，因为这些区域在城市星系模型中位于质量中心附近，便于向圣淘沙、滨海湾和动物园进行低能耗轨道转移。4晚酒店约5000–7500元；景点门票与交通餐饮约7000–8500元，整体预算约19000–24000元。住宿优先选靠近MRT的酒店，因为MRT可视为稳定椭圆轨道，能减少亲子出行中的随机扰动。\n\n第1天：广州飞新加坡，抵达后办理入住。下午轻松游滨海湾区域，可安排鱼尾狮公园、滨海湾花园外部区域和夜晚灯光秀。从天体物理角度看，第一天属于低亮度主序阶段，不宜安排高能爆发项目，应让家庭系统完成重力适应。晚餐推荐去滨海湾或金沙美食广场，选择海南鸡饭、叻沙、肉骨茶等本地餐，孩子选择也多。当天不建议排太多项目，以免行程像超新星一样提前耗尽能量。\n\n第2天：安排圣淘沙一日亲子游。上午进入新加坡环球影城，优先玩适合家庭的项目，如变形金刚、芝麻街、马达加斯加、侏罗纪河流探险等。环球影城在本行程中相当于红巨星阶段，能量密度最高，应放在家庭体力仍处于高温高压状态时。下午可根据孩子体力选择继续环球影城，或转去S.E.A.海洋馆，使行程从高能恒星逐步冷却为蓝色星云。交通可从市区坐MRT到HarbourFront，再转圣淘沙捷运或步行入岛，这相当于从内轨道切换到外轨道。晚餐推荐VivoCity内的亲子友好餐厅，如鼎泰丰、莆田、Food Republic，选择多且交通方便。\n\n第3天：安排新加坡动物园亲子日。动物园在结构上接近生态主序星，稳定、持续、适合长时间观测。建议上午出发，乘MRT到Khatib站后转Mandai Shuttle，或直接Grab打车作为短程火箭助推。白天游新加坡动物园，重点看红毛猩猩、白虎、长颈鹿、儿童雨林乐园等；若孩子精力充足，可加河川生态园，形成双星观测系统，但不建议同一天再排夜间动物园，否则会造成潮汐撕裂式疲劳。午餐可在园区内解决，晚餐回市区吃松发肉骨茶、亚坤或商场餐厅。\n\n第4天：上午去滨海湾花园，重点安排云雾林和花穹。云雾林可理解为人工星云，花穹类似封闭生态舱，室内有空调，适合亲子避暑。下午可去科学艺术博物馆或新加坡科学中心二选一：若从文艺复兴透视法看，科学艺术博物馆更像画面中的穹顶焦点；若从实验物理看，新加坡科学中心更适合孩子互动。晚上可逛乌节路或武吉士，安排轻松购物和亲子餐厅，使整幅旅行画面完成最后一层罩染。\n\n第5天：根据航班时间，上午可去牛车水、哈芝巷或酒店附近商场简单逛逛，购买斑斓蛋糕、TWG茶、肉干等伴手礼，然后前往机场。如果时间充裕，樟宜机场的星耀樟宜可作为最后的环形星门停留点，适合亲子拍照和休息。注意事项：新加坡天气炎热潮湿，类似高湿度行星大气层，务必带防晒、雨具和水壶；环球影城和动物园建议提前买票，以锁定观测窗口；公共交通可使用Visa/Mastercard感应支付或购买EZ-Link卡；亲子出行每天控制1个核心大景点，避免家庭轨道系统发生混沌偏移。"}
```

### 1) search  
Rationale: The prompt asks to extend LoRA for continual learning, so I first look for a recent PEFT/LoRA-centered parent work rather than a general LLM survey. Among the candidates, `oa:W4393138529` is the most directly relevant parent.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"parameter efficient fine tuning LoRA continual learning catastrophic forgetting large models survey","year_min":2023,"year_max":2024,"k":5}
```

[result] The most relevant recent parent is `oa:W4393138529` — *Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning for Large Models: A Comprehensive Survey* (2024). It covers LoRA and related PEFT methods, making it a strong lineage parent for a continual-learning extension.

---

### 2) read  
Rationale: I next fetch the parent details to ground the child idea in the paper’s actual scope: PEFT for large models, including LoRA’s low-rank adaptation mechanism and its efficiency motivation.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4393138529"}
```

[result] `oa:W4393138529` surveys parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods for large models, including LoRA, adapters, prompt tuning, prefix tuning, and related variants. The paper emphasizes reducing trainable parameters and memory costs while preserving downstream-task performance, but it treats continual multi-task learning and catastrophic forgetting mostly as adjacent challenges rather than the central mechanism.

---

### 3) extract_genome  
Rationale: To build a lineage-aware proposal, I extract the parent’s “genome”: its core mechanism, target niche, representation, training signal, memory strategy, and evaluation style.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"oa:W4393138529"}
```

[result] Extracted parent genome for `oa:W4393138529`:

```json
{
  "mechanism": "Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of frozen large models using small trainable modules such as LoRA low-rank updates, adapters, prompts, and prefixes.",
  "niche": "Efficient adaptation of large pretrained models to downstream tasks under compute, memory, and storage constraints.",
  "representation": "Task-specific lightweight parameter blocks, especially low-rank matrices injected into selected neural network layers.",
  "training_signal": "Standard supervised or instruction-tuning losses on individual downstream tasks.",
  "memory_strategy": "Reduce trainable and stored parameters per task; not primarily designed for continual learning or forgetting prevention.",
  "evaluation": "Compare task performance, trainable parameter count, memory footprint, computational cost, and sometimes inference latency."
}
```

---

### 4) propose  
Rationale: Building on `oa:W4393138529`, the child proposal should keep LoRA’s efficiency but add a continual-learning mechanism: task accumulation, interference control, and reuse without catastrophic forgetting.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism":"Continual Orthogonal LoRA: maintain a shared library of low-rank basis adapters and learn sparse task-specific routing coefficients, while enforcing approximate orthogonality between new task subspaces and high-importance previous subspaces.","niche":"Continual learning for large language models across many sequential tasks where full retraining, full model copies, or large replay buffers are impractical.","representation":"A frozen backbone plus a compositional LoRA memory: reusable low-rank basis matrices, task keys, sparse router coefficients, and per-basis importance scores.","training_signal":"Task loss on the current task plus orthogonality regularization, importance-weighted consolidation, and optional distillation from frozen previous task adapter compositions.","memory_strategy":"Store a compact adapter basis bank rather than one full LoRA per task; protect important bases using Fisher or gradient-based importance and allocate new bases only when routing conflict is high.","evaluation":"Sequential multi-task benchmarks measuring average accuracy, forgetting, forward transfer, backward transfer, adapter memory growth, and inference-time routing overhead."}}
```

[result] Draft child idea: extend LoRA from static per-task adaptation into a continual adapter-subspace system. Instead of saving an independent LoRA module for every task, the model learns reusable low-rank bases and task-conditioned sparse compositions while regularizing new updates away from important prior subspaces.

---

### 5) genome_diff  
Rationale: I compute the delta against parent `oa:W4393138529` to make the child contribution explicit: the novelty should be more than “use LoRA”; it should add continual memory, subspace routing, and forgetting control.

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","parent_id":"oa:W4393138529","proposed_genome":{"mechanism":"Continual Orthogonal LoRA: maintain a shared library of low-rank basis adapters and learn sparse task-specific routing coefficients, while enforcing approximate orthogonality between new task subspaces and high-importance previous subspaces.","niche":"Continual learning for large language models across many sequential tasks where full retraining, full model copies, or large replay buffers are impractical.","representation":"A frozen backbone plus a compositional LoRA memory: reusable low-rank basis matrices, task keys, sparse router coefficients, and per-basis importance scores.","training_signal":"Task loss on the current task plus orthogonality regularization, importance-weighted consolidation, and optional distillation from frozen previous task adapter compositions.","memory_strategy":"Store a compact adapter basis bank rather than one full LoRA per task; protect important bases using Fisher or gradient-based importance and allocate new bases only when routing conflict is high.","evaluation":"Sequential multi-task benchmarks measuring average accuracy, forgetting, forward transfer, backward transfer, adapter memory growth, and inference-time routing overhead."}}
```

[result] Key delta from `oa:W4393138529`: the parent surveys PEFT/LoRA for efficient per-task adaptation, while the child introduces a continual-learning controller over LoRA subspaces. The main new genes are: reusable low-rank basis memory, sparse task routing, orthogonality-based interference avoidance, importance-weighted consolidation, and explicit forgetting/transfer evaluation.

---

### 6) novelty_check  
Rationale: Because there are existing works on continual learning, adapters, and LoRA variants, I check whether the precise mechanism — sparse routed reusable LoRA basis bank with orthogonal subspace allocation and consolidation — appears as an already-established method.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"continual learning with LoRA using sparse task-conditioned routing over reusable low-rank adapter bases, orthogonality constraints against important prior subspaces, and importance-weighted consolidation to prevent catastrophic forgetting","year_min":2023,"year_max":2026}
```

[result] Related ideas exist separately: LoRA for PEFT, adapter-based continual learning, orthogonal gradient/subspace constraints, and regularization-based consolidation. The combined mechanism appears plausibly novel as a unified continual LoRA framework that dynamically reuses or allocates low-rank bases based on routing conflict and importance.

---

### 7) final propose  
Rationale: I refine the proposal to emphasize the concrete algorithmic contribution and evaluation plan, while preserving lineage from `oa:W4393138529`.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism":"OrthoRoute-LoRA: a continual PEFT algorithm that decomposes LoRA updates into a growing bank of reusable low-rank basis adapters. For each new task, a lightweight router selects a sparse mixture of existing bases; if interference is detected, the method allocates a new basis constrained to be approximately orthogonal to high-importance prior bases. Important bases are protected by Fisher- or gradient-importance consolidation and optional adapter-level distillation.","niche":"Sequential adaptation of large language models to many tasks, domains, or user-specific skills under tight storage and compute budgets, where catastrophic forgetting and unbounded per-task adapter growth are unacceptable.","representation":"Frozen pretrained backbone; shared LoRA basis bank; sparse task or input-conditioned routing vectors; task keys or domain descriptors; per-basis importance statistics; optional compact exemplars or synthetic logits for distillation without large replay buffers.","training_signal":"Current-task supervised or instruction-tuning loss combined with three continual objectives: orthogonality loss to reduce subspace interference, consolidation loss to preserve important prior bases, and distillation loss to maintain previous adapter behavior when task identifiers or validation probes are available.","memory_strategy":"Bounded compositional adapter memory: reuse existing LoRA bases when compatible, allocate new low-rank bases only under measured routing conflict, prune or merge rarely used bases, and store importance statistics rather than full historical datasets or full model checkpoints.","evaluation":"Evaluate on sequential NLP and instruction-following task streams using average final performance, forgetting, forward transfer, backward transfer, parameter growth per task, routing latency, memory footprint, and ablations over orthogonality, routing sparsity, consolidation, and basis allocation thresholds."}}
```

[result] Final proposal: build on the LoRA/PEFT foundation surveyed in `oa:W4393138529` by turning LoRA into a continual, compositional, interference-aware memory system. The central hypothesis is that catastrophic forgetting can be reduced without full replay or full model copies by routing each new task through reusable low-rank subspaces and protecting important prior adapter directions.

full_prompt: Can nanomaterials improve performance in next-generation electronic devices?
chosen:
The prompt has a **vague_domain ambiguity**: “next-generation electronic devices” could mean CMOS logic, memory, sensors, displays, wearables, quantum devices, or power electronics. I’ll assume the domain is **flexible/wearable electronics**, because nanomaterials are especially relevant where high electrical performance must be combined with mechanical flexibility.  

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"nanomaterials improve performance flexible wearable electronics graphene MXene carbon nanotubes silver nanowires review"}
```  

[result] Reviews and recent studies commonly identify graphene, carbon nanotubes, MXenes, metal nanowires, quantum dots, and 2D semiconductors as promising for flexible/wearable electronics because they can improve conductivity, sensitivity, transparency, stretchability, and low-power operation, though manufacturability, stability, toxicity, and device-to-device variability remain major barriers.  

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "niche_genome": "Assumption/scope: evaluate nanomaterials for flexible and wearable next-generation electronic devices, including stretchable interconnects, thin-film transistors, and physical/chemical sensors. This does not cover quantum computing, conventional CMOS scaling, batteries, or high-power electronics.",
    "hypothesis_genome": "Nanomaterials can improve flexible/wearable device performance by combining high carrier mobility or conductivity with mechanical compliance, high surface-area interfaces, and tunable nanoscale electronic structure.",
    "material_genome": "Candidate materials include graphene, carbon nanotubes, MXenes, silver/copper nanowires, MoS2 and other 2D semiconductors, quantum dots, and hybrid nanocomposites with elastomers or conductive polymers.",
    "performance_genome": "Key metrics to compare are electrical conductivity, carrier mobility, on/off ratio, sensitivity, response time, power consumption, optical transparency, bend/stretch durability, and long-term environmental stability.",
    "mechanism_genome": "Expected gains arise from percolating conductive networks, quantum confinement, high surface-to-volume ratio for sensing, tunable bandgaps in 2D materials, and strain-tolerant architectures that preserve charge transport under deformation.",
    "limitation_genome": "Under this flexible/wearable-electronics assumption, the main unresolved issues are scalable low-cost fabrication, material uniformity, contact resistance, oxidation or moisture instability, nanomaterial toxicity, integration with existing manufacturing, and reliability over repeated mechanical cycling."
  }
}
```?sia

full_prompt: As a safety analyst in an autonomous vehicle company, how can we predict when human operators will lose situational awareness during handoff events and design alerts that restore attention effectively?
chosen:
I’ll first map the recent autonomous-vehicle handoff literature around takeover readiness, situational awareness, and alert modality, then inspect papers that combine prediction with intervention design. The goal is to turn findings into an industry-facing mechanism: detect loss of situation awareness before takeover and trigger alerts calibrated to restore orienting without overloading the operator.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"autonomous vehicle takeover request situational awareness driver monitoring alert modality human operator handoff","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: The search returns several high-fit papers. Top candidates include OpenAlex `W2963447072`, Zhang et al. 2019, “Determinants of take-over time from automated driving: A meta-analysis of 129 studies”; `W3090840135`, Lu et al. 2020, on multimodal takeover requests and driver state; `W4285486772`, Du et al. 2022, on physiological and glance-based prediction of takeover performance; `W4312326658`, Körber et al. 2023, on driver monitoring for conditionally automated driving; and `W3036769238`, Gold et al.-lineage work on takeover request timing and non-driving-related tasks. The strongest evidence cluster links non-driving task engagement, eyes-off-road duration, lead time, traffic complexity, and alert modality to takeover quality.
The meta-analysis is useful as a baseline because it aggregates determinants of takeover timing and performance across many simulator studies. I’ll read it to identify robust predictors that an AV safety team could operationalize.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W2963447072"}
```

[result]: Zhang et al. synthesize takeover studies and find that takeover time and quality are systematically affected by available lead time, non-driving-related task type, automation exposure, urgency, and scenario complexity. Visual-manual tasks and long eyes-off-road periods degrade takeover performance, while longer warning lead times improve reaction but do not guarantee safe control if the driver lacks scene comprehension. The paper emphasizes that raw reaction time is insufficient: lane keeping, braking smoothness, collision risk, and hazard recognition better capture safe recovery of situational awareness. For industry use, it suggests predicting “takeover readiness” as a multi-factor state rather than relying on a fixed countdown alert.
Next I’ll inspect a more recent driver-state prediction paper because the industry problem asks not only “what predicts loss of awareness” but how to detect it during handoff events. I’m looking for sensor features and model targets that can be implemented in a vehicle monitoring stack.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W4285486772"}
```

[result]: Du et al. report that multimodal driver-state features—eye gaze, blink behavior, head pose, heart-rate variability, and non-driving task indicators—predict takeover performance better than any single channel. The most informative features are typically recent gaze allocation to the forward roadway, mirror/instrument-cluster scanning, physiological arousal changes near the request, and task disengagement latency. Their models distinguish quick motor response from higher-quality takeover, showing that a driver can grab the wheel quickly while still lacking hazard-level situation awareness. The paper recommends adaptive takeover requests that vary lead time and modality according to inferred readiness and environmental risk.
To convert the evidence into a designable hypothesis, I’ll extract the implied “genome” from the prediction paper: mechanism, niche, observations, limitations, and claims. This helps identify what can be extended for an AV-company safety workflow.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"W4285486772"}
```

[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = “multimodal driver-state sensing predicts takeover readiness through gaze, posture, physiology, and task engagement”; niche = “conditionally automated driving handoffs after non-driving engagement”; observation = “eyes-off-road and low scan diversity before takeover predict poorer control and hazard response”; limitation = “models often predict post-hoc simulator outcomes and may not prescribe the best alert to restore awareness”; delta = “move from detection of impairment to adaptive attention-restoration intervention”; claim = “readiness-aware alerts can improve takeover quality compared with fixed warnings.” The key gap is that many systems classify readiness but do not close the loop by selecting the least disruptive alert that rebuilds situation awareness.
Before proposing, I’ll check whether the specific mechanism—closed-loop prediction of situation-awareness decay plus adaptive alert selection—is already established or remains a novel synthesis. This matters because a practical industry proposal should avoid merely restating driver monitoring.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"closed-loop situational-awareness decay prediction using gaze/physiology/task/context features, followed by adaptive multimodal alerts selected to restore scene comprehension during autonomous-vehicle handoff","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```

[result]: The mechanism is partially represented in adjacent work: driver monitoring models predict takeover readiness, and separate HMI studies compare auditory, visual, haptic, speech, and multimodal takeover requests. However, fewer papers explicitly model “situational-awareness decay” as a latent state and use that state to choose alert content, modality, and escalation sequence. The novelty opportunity is to integrate prediction and intervention: estimate whether the operator lacks perceptual, comprehension, or projection-level awareness, then deliver alerts that target that missing layer. This appears meaningfully differentiated from fixed takeover requests or generic fatigue/distraction alerts.
The evidence points to an actionable product concept: predict latent situational-awareness decay, not just distraction, and use the prediction to select alert strategies that rebuild awareness before control transfer. I’ll now propose a gene genome tailored to an AV safety analyst’s industry problem.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Model operator situational awareness as a latent, time-varying readiness state with three layers: perceptual awareness of the road scene, comprehension of current hazards and automation intent, and projection of near-future conflict. Estimate this state continuously from gaze distribution, eyes-off-road time, head pose, blink dynamics, steering/wheel contact, heart-rate variability or skin conductance when available, non-driving task engagement, automation mode history, traffic complexity, time-to-collision margins, and takeover lead time. When predicted awareness falls below a risk-adjusted threshold, trigger an adaptive alert policy that chooses modality, urgency, content, and escalation to restore the missing awareness layer rather than merely prompting hand placement.","niche_genome":"Conditionally or highly automated vehicles with human fallback operators, especially SAE Level 2+/Level 3 handoff events, remote safety-supervisor stations, fleet test operations, and commercial AV deployments where operators may monitor automation for long periods and become out-of-the-loop. The highest-value niche is safety-critical handoff under moderate-to-high environmental complexity: construction zones, emergency vehicles, unusual agent behavior, degraded perception confidence, bad weather, or minimum-risk-maneuver decisions.","observation_genome":"Recent takeover literature shows that takeover quality is degraded by visual-manual non-driving tasks, long eyes-off-road intervals, low roadway scan diversity, short warning lead time, high traffic density, and poor comprehension of why automation is requesting help. Simulator and driver-monitoring studies also show that fast motor response is not equivalent to safe takeover: operators may touch the wheel quickly while failing to identify the relevant hazard, choose the wrong maneuver, or overcorrect. Multimodal features combining gaze, posture, physiology, task context, and scene risk predict takeover quality better than single-channel distraction measures.","limitation_genome":"Existing driver-monitoring systems often classify distraction, drowsiness, or takeover readiness but do not explicitly infer which layer of situational awareness is missing. Many takeover-request designs use fixed audio/visual/haptic alerts or simple escalation timers, which can either under-inform confused operators or overload already-stressed ones. Much evidence comes from simulator studies, so deployment requires calibration against naturalistic fleet data, careful false-alarm management, privacy-preserving sensing, and validation that alerts improve hazard comprehension rather than merely reducing reaction time.","delta_genome":"Build a closed-loop handoff safety module: first, a predictive model outputs a calibrated probability of awareness loss and decomposes it into perception/comprehension/projection deficits; second, an alert-selection policy maps the deficit and scene risk to intervention type. For perceptual loss, use orienting cues such as seat/steering haptics plus directional visual highlighting. For comprehension loss, use concise speech or HUD text explaining the reason for handoff, e.g., 'construction barrels blocking lane ahead.' For projection loss, show or speak the recommended immediate action and time budget, e.g., 'prepare to brake; lead vehicle stopped in 3 seconds.' Escalate only when the operator fails to reorient, and use post-alert gaze/hazard-check behavior to verify recovery before transferring authority.","claim_genome":"An AV handoff system that predicts latent situational-awareness decay and delivers deficit-targeted multimodal alerts will produce safer handoffs than fixed takeover requests. It should improve hazard recognition, gaze return to task-relevant regions, braking/steering smoothness, and collision-avoidance performance, while reducing nuisance alarms and cognitive overload. The core industry metric should be not just takeover reaction time but verified awareness recovery: evidence that the operator has looked at the relevant hazard, understood the automation limitation, and demonstrated a safe control plan before or during authority transfer."}}
```

[result]: Proposed concept: a readiness-aware handoff assistant that forecasts when an operator is out of the loop and adapts alerts to rebuild situational awareness. The design shifts the safety objective from “make the human respond quickly” to “make the human understand the scene quickly enough to act safely.” A validation plan would compare fixed takeover requests against adaptive awareness-targeted alerts using hazard recognition, gaze-to-hazard latency, control smoothness, minimum TTC, and near-miss rates in simulator, closed-course, and monitored fleet trials.-vesm_FINALုတ်

Top candidates include OpenAlex `W2963447072`, Zhang et al. 2019, “Determinants of take-over time from automated driving: A meta-analysis of 129 studies”; `W3090840135`, Lu et al. 2020, on multimodal takeover requests and driver state; `W4285486772`, Du et al. 2022, on physiological and glance-based prediction of takeover performance; `W4312326658`, Körber et al. 2023, on driver monitoring for conditionally automated driving; and `W3036769238`, Gold et al.-lineage work on takeover request timing and non-driving-related tasks. The strongest evidence cluster links non-driving task engagement, eyes-off-road duration, lead time, traffic complexity, and alert modality to takeover quality.
The meta-analysis is useful as a baseline because it aggregates determinants of takeover timing and performance across many simulator studies. I’ll read it to identify robust predictors that an AV safety team could operationalize.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W2963447072"}
```

[result]: Zhang et al. synthesize takeover studies and find that takeover time and quality are systematically affected by available lead time, non-driving-related task type, automation exposure, urgency, and scenario complexity. Visual-manual tasks and long eyes-off-road periods degrade takeover performance, while longer warning lead times improve reaction but do not guarantee safe control if the driver lacks scene comprehension. The paper emphasizes that raw reaction time is insufficient: lane keeping, braking smoothness, collision risk, and hazard recognition better capture safe recovery of situational awareness. For industry use, it suggests predicting “takeover readiness” as a multi-factor state rather than relying on a fixed countdown alert.
Next I’ll inspect a more recent driver-state prediction paper because the industry problem asks not only “what predicts loss of awareness” but how to detect it during handoff events. I’m looking for sensor features and model targets that can be implemented in a vehicle monitoring stack.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W4285486772"}
```

[result]: Du et al. report that multimodal driver-state features—eye gaze, blink behavior, head pose, heart-rate variability, and non-driving task indicators—predict takeover performance better than any single channel. The most informative features are typically recent gaze allocation to the forward roadway, mirror/instrument-cluster scanning, physiological arousal changes near the request, and task disengagement latency. Their models distinguish quick motor response from higher-quality takeover, showing that a driver can grab the wheel quickly while still lacking hazard-level situation awareness. The paper recommends adaptive takeover requests that vary lead time and modality according to inferred readiness and environmental risk.
To convert the evidence into a designable hypothesis, I’ll extract the implied “genome” from the prediction paper: mechanism, niche, observations, limitations, and claims. This helps identify what can be extended for an AV-company safety workflow.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"W4285486772"}
```

[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = “multimodal driver-state sensing predicts takeover readiness through gaze, posture, physiology, and task engagement”; niche = “conditionally automated driving handoffs after non-driving engagement”; observation = “eyes-off-road and low scan diversity before takeover predict poorer control and hazard response”; limitation = “models often predict post-hoc simulator outcomes and may not prescribe the best alert to restore awareness”; delta = “move from detection of impairment to adaptive attention-restoration intervention”; claim = “readiness-aware alerts can improve takeover quality compared with fixed warnings.” The key gap is that many systems classify readiness but do not close the loop by selecting the least disruptive alert that rebuilds situation awareness.
Before proposing, I’ll check whether the specific mechanism—closed-loop prediction of situation-awareness decay plus adaptive alert selection—is already established or remains a novel synthesis. This matters because a practical industry proposal should avoid merely restating driver monitoring.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"closed-loop situational-awareness decay prediction using gaze/physiology/task/context features, followed by adaptive multimodal alerts selected to restore scene comprehension during autonomous-vehicle handoff","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```

[result]: The mechanism is partially represented in adjacent work: driver monitoring models predict takeover readiness, and separate HMI studies compare auditory, visual, haptic, speech, and multimodal takeover requests. However, fewer papers explicitly model “situational-awareness decay” as a latent state and use that state to choose alert content, modality, and escalation sequence. The novelty opportunity is to integrate prediction and intervention: estimate whether the operator lacks perceptual, comprehension, or projection-level awareness, then deliver alerts that target that missing layer. This appears meaningfully differentiated from fixed takeover requests or generic fatigue/distraction alerts.
The evidence points to an actionable product concept: predict latent situational-awareness decay, not just distraction, and use the prediction to select alert strategies that rebuild awareness before control transfer. I’ll now propose a gene genome tailored to an AV safety analyst’s industry problem.

full_prompt: Develop approximation algorithms for submodular maximization under matroid intersection constraints in the streaming model. Analyze whether adaptivity can be reduced without worsening the competitive ratio beyond current lower bounds.

candidates.title: Scalable and space-efficient Robust Matroid Center algorithms
candidates.snippet: Abstract Given a dataset V of points from some metric space, a popular robust formulation of the k -center clustering problem requires to select k points (centers) of V which minimize the maximum distance of any point of V from its closest

chosen:
Rationale: First locate the closest known results: streaming submodular maximization under multiple matroid-type constraints, and parallel/adaptive lower bounds for submodular maximization.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"streaming monotone submodular maximization matroid intersection p matroid constraints adaptivity lower bounds"}
```  
[result] Simulated: Search returns clusters around (i) threshold/greedy streaming for monotone submodular maximization under cardinality and matroid constraints; (ii) streaming algorithms for \(p\)-matchoids/\(p\)-systems with approximation degrading as \(\Theta(p)\); (iii) adaptivity lower bounds for submodular maximization showing that constant-factor approximation generally requires \(\Omega(\log n/\log\log n)\) adaptive value-query rounds in parallel-query models unless the algorithm uses exponentially many queries; and (iv) matroid-intersection offline algorithms based on exchange graphs/continuous greedy, not directly streaming.

Rationale: The provided candidate is matroid-related; read it to determine whether its space-efficient matroid machinery transfers to submodular streaming or is orthogonal.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4366087049"}
```  
[result] Simulated: The paper “Scalable and space-efficient Robust Matroid Center algorithms” studies robust clustering/center objectives with matroid constraints. It uses matroid feasibility and coresets/space reduction, but the objective is metric center-style rather than submodular value-oracle maximization. It does not provide a streaming approximation for monotone submodular maximization under matroid intersection, but its space-efficient treatment of matroid independence oracles suggests maintaining compact candidate sets may be viable.

Rationale: Formulate an algorithmic direction by combining thresholded streaming, matroid-intersection exchange feasibility, and batched/low-adaptivity marginal estimation, then compare against known adaptivity lower-bound barriers.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"matroid intersection streaming exchange graph submodular threshold greedy low adaptivity"}
```  
[result] Simulated: Relevant techniques include: maintaining a feasible set under the intersection of \(p\) matroids using local exchange; using geometrically spaced marginal thresholds to avoid knowing \(\mathrm{OPT}\); retaining short exchange certificates/candidate buffers; and reducing adaptivity by evaluating marginal gains for batches against a frozen solution. Known barriers suggest that reducing adaptive rounds below logarithmic or polylogarithmic levels while preserving a constant approximation is unlikely in the general value-oracle model.

propose:
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Design a one-pass threshold-exchange streaming algorithm for monotone submodular maximization over \\(\\mathcal I=\\bigcap_{j=1}^p\\mathcal I_j\\), where each \\(\\mathcal I_j\\) is a matroid accessed by an independence oracle. Maintain \\(O(\\varepsilon^{-1}\\log M)\\) candidate solutions indexed by geometric thresholds \\(\\tau\\), where \\(M\\) brackets \\(\\mathrm{OPT}\\). On arrival of element \\(e\\), compute its marginal \\(\\Delta(e\\mid S_\\tau)\\). If \\(S_\\tau+e\\in\\mathcal I\\) and \\(\\Delta(e\\mid S_\\tau)\\ge\\tau\\), insert it. If infeasible, compute a bounded exchange set \\(X\\subseteq S_\\tau\\) certified by the matroid-intersection exchange graph and insert \\(e\\) only when \\(\\Delta(e\\mid S_\\tau)-\\sum_{x\\in X}\\widehat\\Delta(x)\\ge\\tau\\), deleting \\(X\\). Use lazy marginal upper bounds \\(\\widehat\\Delta(x)\\) to charge discarded value to accepted value. A batched variant freezes \\(S_\\tau\\) for blocks of size \\(B\\), evaluates all marginals in the block nonadaptively, and then applies a deterministic exchange-resolution rule.","niche_genome":"Streaming value-oracle algorithms for monotone submodular maximization under matroid-intersection constraints, especially when \\(p\\) is small or when each arriving element participates in a bounded number of exchange conflicts. The target model is insertion-only, one pass, polynomial independence-oracle calls, and semi-streaming space \\(\\widetilde O(rp/\\varepsilon)\\), where \\(r\\) is the common rank scale.","observation_genome":"For \\(p\\)-matroid intersections, a pure accept-if-feasible threshold algorithm loses value because high-marginal elements arriving late may be blocked by earlier lower-marginal elements. Exchange-based streaming can recover part of this loss: each removed element can be charged to an inserted element, and each inserted element is charged at most \\(p\\) times through the matroid circuits. This suggests an approximation of the form \\((p+1+O(\\varepsilon))^{-1}\\) in favorable exchange regimes, or more conservatively \\(\\Theta(1/p)\\), matching the dependence typical for streaming algorithms over \\(p\\)-systems and \\(p\\)-matchoids. The threshold grid removes the need for knowing \\(\\mathrm{OPT}\\).","limitation_genome":"The proposed algorithm is unlikely to beat known constant-factor lower-bound phenomena for general submodular value-oracle maximization with substantially sublogarithmic adaptivity. In particular, batching marginals against frozen solutions introduces stale marginal estimates; for arbitrary submodular functions, adversarial curvature can make aggressive batching lose a constant factor. Thus one should not expect \\(O(1)\\)-adaptive or fully nonadaptive streaming updates to preserve the best \\(\\Theta(1/p)\\) ratio without additional assumptions such as bounded curvature, random-order streams, or special matroid structure.","delta_genome":"The main technical delta is to replace standard streaming greedy for a single matroid/cardinality constraint by a matroid-intersection exchange filter with amortized submodular charging, while separately parameterizing adaptivity via block size. The analysis would prove a tradeoff: as the number of adaptive marginal-update phases decreases from \\(O(n)\\) to \\(O(\\log n/\\varepsilon)\\) or block-adaptive \\(O(n/B)\\), the approximation degrades by only an additive \\(O(\\varepsilon)\\) term, provided stale marginal errors are controlled by refreshing thresholds geometrically.","claim_genome":"A plausible theorem is: for fixed \\(p\\), there is a one-pass streaming algorithm using \\(\\widetilde O(rp/\\varepsilon)\\) space and polynomial oracle calls that returns \\(S\\in\\bigcap_{j=1}^p\\mathcal I_j\\) with \\(f(S)\\ge (c_p-O(\\varepsilon))\\mathrm{OPT}\\), where \\(c_p=\\Theta(1/p)\\), via thresholded exchange. Moreover, adaptivity can be reduced to polylogarithmic marginal-evaluation rounds by batching and lazy thresholds without asymptotically worsening \\(c_p\\); however, reducing adaptivity below the known \\(\\Omega(\\log n/\\log\\log n)\\)-type value-oracle barriers while keeping a constant approximation would require assumptions beyond general monotone submodularity and arbitrary matroid intersections."}}

propose:
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Model the arriving elements as planetesimals entering a protoplanetary disk and choose a solution by simulating orbital capture under resonant gravitational locking. Treat each matroid constraint as a distinct stellar-magnetic resonance band, and allow an element into the solution only if its assigned orbit remains inside the combined Hill stability region of all bands. When an arriving element violates feasibility, compute a gravitational scattering event: eject the lowest-angular-momentum bodies from the current set and insert the new body if its simulated accretion luminosity exceeds the lost luminosity by a threshold. Maintain geometrically spaced temperature shells rather than value thresholds, and periodically cool the disk according to a radiative-transfer schedule so that high-energy objects migrate inward. A batched variant freezes the disk potential for several arrivals, computes Keplerian trajectories independently, and then resolves collisions using a deterministic Roche-limit rule.","niche_genome":"Streaming value-oracle algorithms for monotone submodular maximization under matroid-intersection constraints, but interpreted through astrophysical disk accretion and orbital-resonance dynamics. The target setting is still insertion-only and one pass, yet the selection procedure is driven by simulated gravitational capture, angular momentum conservation, and thermal cooling rather than combinatorial exchange or submodular marginal analysis.","observation_genome":"Late high-value elements can be viewed as massive bodies arriving after a disk has already formed; instead of being blocked by previous low-value elements, they may destabilize existing orbits and cause ejections. By analogy with planetary migration, each inserted element should clear a local resonance zone and absorb or expel nearby competitors. This suggests that approximation quality is governed by the ratio of stable orbital shells to resonance conflicts, with performance degrading as additional matroid constraints act like extra perturbing planets. The threshold grid is replaced by a temperature-radius ladder that supposedly brackets the optimal accretion luminosity.","limitation_genome":"The proposal is not justified by standard submodular optimization theory because Newtonian orbital stability, Hill spheres, and radiative cooling do not imply feasibility or approximation guarantees for arbitrary matroid intersections. Adversarial streams need not resemble protoplanetary disks, and submodular marginal values do not obey conservation of angular momentum. Any claimed low-adaptivity improvement based on freezing the gravitational potential would therefore be vulnerable to stale marginal estimates and would not overcome known value-oracle lower bounds without additional non-combinatorial assumptions.","delta_genome":"The main technical delta is to replace thresholded matroid-intersection exchange with an astrophysical accretion filter: elements are ranked by simulated luminosity, conflicts are resolved through orbital ejection, and batching corresponds to evolving a frozen N-body potential over fixed time steps. The intended tradeoff would relate adaptive rounds to disk-cooling epochs, claiming that fewer cooling epochs preserve nearly the same selected mass-energy profile, although this does not correspond to a valid submodular charging argument.","claim_genome":"A speculative theorem would state that for fixed \(p\), a one-pass streaming procedure using \(\widetilde O(rp/\varepsilon)\) space can select a feasible set by maintaining \(p\) coupled resonance shells and ejecting unstable bodies according to Hill-stability certificates, achieving \(f(S)\ge (c_p-O(\varepsilon))\mathrm{OPT}\) with \(c_p=\Theta(1/p)\). The adaptivity would allegedly be reducible to polylogarithmic disk-cooling phases by freezing the gravitational potential between batches. However, this claim rests on an astrophysical analogy rather than a valid matroid or submodular proof and is therefore topically mismatched to the original algorithmic problem."}}

full_prompt: 以轨道交通站点周边 TOD 开发为主题，设计一个城市规划科研提示词，要求按 gene_genome 格式输出，包含 6 个字段。
chosen:
步骤1：先扫描TOD在城市规划中的主流研究脉络，明确“站点—片区—网络”三个尺度下常见议题，避免提示词只停留在土地混合或密度描述。  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"urban planning TOD rail transit station area development built environment accessibility land value equity"}
```  
[result]: 检索结果显示，TOD研究高频主题包括“5D建成环境、站点可达性、土地开发强度、公共空间品质、住房可负担性、地价资本化、通勤行为变化”。近年趋势从单一站点开发评价转向“多站点网络协同、社会公平、实施时序和治理机制”。

步骤2：进一步读取实践型资料，提取城市规划研究中可操作的评价维度，尤其关注站点周边800米步行圈与轨道线网的关系。  
```action
{"tool":"read","source":"ITDP TOD Standard; World Bank urban rail and TOD planning guidance; metropolitan station-area planning guidelines","focus":"station-area TOD indicators and planning implementation variables"}
```  
[result]: 实践资料强调八类要素：步行、骑行、公共交通换乘、混合用地、紧凑开发、公共空间、停车管控和社会包容。工具提示：仅以“站点半径”界定TOD容易忽略街网阻隔、土地权属碎片化和开发时序差异。

步骤3：将既有TOD研究抽象成gene_genome，形成可比较的“主流基因组”。  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","text":"Mainstream TOD research explains how rail station proximity, density, diversity, design and destination accessibility affect ridership, land value, travel behavior and urban redevelopment outcomes.","schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: 提取结果显示：mechanism多聚焦“轨道可达性提升—土地价值上升—高强度混合开发—公共交通使用增加”；niche常为大城市轨道站点周边；observation常用POI、人口、地价、路网和客流数据；limitation集中在因果识别不足、站点异质性处理不足和公平影响不足。

步骤4：补充搜索当前可用的新型观测数据，以便把科研提示词设计为可执行的实证研究，而不是概念性框架。  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"TOD station area multisource urban data mobile phone signaling smart card POI street view land parcel planning control"}
```  
[result]: 可用数据包括轨道刷卡/进出站客流、手机信令、POI、建筑轮廓、地块出让与控规、街景图像、房价租金、公共服务设施和步行网络。工具建议将“规划控制指标”和“实际开发结果”并置，以识别TOD实施偏差。

步骤5：比较主流TOD研究基因与可形成创新的研究基因，寻找delta。  
```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","baseline_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"station proximity and built environment affect ridership and development intensity","niche_genome":"large metropolitan rail station areas","observation_genome":"density, land use mix, road network, ridership, land value","limitation_genome":"mostly cross-sectional correlation, weak treatment of governance, equity and implementation lag","delta_genome":"limited","claim_genome":"TOD improves transit use and compact development"},"candidate_shift":"focus on mismatch between planned TOD intensity and realized station-area development, mediated by accessibility, land assembly, street permeability and public-service equity"}
```  
[result]: 差异点集中在三方面：一是从“是否靠近轨道”转向“规划承诺—开发实现”的缺口；二是从平均效应转向站点类型异质性；三是把公平性和公共服务可达性纳入TOD绩效，而非仅看客流与地价。

步骤6：进行新颖性检查，判断该研究提示词是否具备清晰问题意识和城市规划学科贡献。  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Evaluate station-area TOD development by measuring the gap between planned development potential and realized built outcomes, and explain the gap through accessibility, parcel fragmentation, governance constraints, market demand and equity-oriented service provision.","field":"urban planning"}
```

Use weakly informative priors such as beta,alpha_0,mu_tau ~ N(0,A^2), sigma,sigma_alpha,sigma_tau ~ half-t_nu(0,s), preferably implemented with a non-centered parameterization tau_j = mu_tau + sigma_tau eta_j, eta_j ~ N(0,1). The estimands are the population mean treatment effect mu_tau, the finite-site average J^{-1} sum_j tau_j, and the site-specific effects tau_j.","niche_genome":"This model targets individually randomized or cluster-stratified multi-site trials where treatment is randomized within site and the scientific question includes both the average treatment effect and cross-site treatment-effect heterogeneity. It is appropriate when site-specific sample sizes are moderate to small relative to the number of sites, so that no-pooling estimates are unstable and partial pooling is attractive.","observation_genome":"If the site-level no-pooling estimator is hat_tau_j with approximate sampling variance v_j, and if mu_tau and sigma_tau^2 are treated as known, the posterior mean has the shrinkage form E[tau_j | hat_tau_j] = w_j hat_tau_j + (1-w_j) mu_tau, where w_j = sigma_tau^2/(sigma_tau^2+v_j). Conditional on a fixed true tau_j, its design expectation is E_Z,Y[E(tau_j | data)] - tau_j = -(1-w_j)(tau_j-mu_tau). Thus extreme positive site effects are biased downward and extreme negative site effects are biased upward. With estimated hyperparameters, the same phenomenon remains, with additional dependence induced by estimation of mu_tau and sigma_tau^2.","limitation_genome":"Posterior credible intervals for tau_j are Bayesianly calibrated under the full exchangeable random-effects model, but they may fail to achieve nominal frequentist coverage conditional on fixed site effects. Coverage for mu_tau or the finite-site average is usually less sensitive but can still be distorted when J is small, the prior on sigma_tau concentrates near zero, treatment allocation is highly imbalanced within sites, or the exchangeability assumption is violated.","delta_genome":"Assess shrinkage-induced bias and frequentist coverage by repeated randomization/sampling simulations. full_prompt: In heliospheric cosmic-ray transport, Parker transport equation solvers and test-particle simulations are widely used. They often simplify turbulence spectra and spatial diffusion tensors, limiting accuracy during solar-cycle variations. Propose a model targeting better prediction of cosmic-ray modulation at Earth using physically constrained turbulence inputs.
chosen:
The prompt already cites the established approaches and the key gap—over-simplified turbulence and diffusion inputs during solar-cycle variability—so I can propose directly.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "name": "Physically Constrained Turbulence-Driven Cosmic-Ray Modulation Model",
    "problem": "Improve prediction of galactic cosmic-ray modulation at Earth by replacing simplified turbulence spectra and diffusion tensors in Parker-transport solvers with solar-cycle-dependent, observationally constrained turbulence inputs.",
    "core_hypothesis": "Cosmic-ray modulation accuracy improves when parallel diffusion, perpendicular diffusion, drift suppression, and adiabatic cooling are computed from evolving heliospheric turbulence quantities constrained by solar-wind and magnetic-field observations rather than prescribed as static empirical coefficients.",
    "model_design": "Couple a Parker transport equation solver to a turbulence module that ingests solar-wind speed, magnetic-field magnitude, magnetic variance, correlation length, cross helicity, residual energy, and heliospheric current-sheet tilt; derive rigidity-, latitude-, radius-, and time-dependent diffusion tensors using quasi-linear/nonlinear guiding-center theory with physically bounded turbulence spectra.",
    "validation_plan": "Calibrate against multi-spacecraft datasets from ACE, Wind, Ulysses, Voyager, Parker Solar Probe, Solar Orbiter, and neutron-monitor records; test across solar minimum, maximum, polarity reversals, Forbush-decrease recovery phases, and radial gradients between 1 AU and the outer heliosphere.",
    "expected_advance": "A predictive framework that links observed heliospheric turbulence evolution to cosmic-ray intensity at Earth, reducing reliance on ad hoc diffusion coefficients and improving forecasts of solar-cycle-dependent cosmic-ray modulation."
  }
}
```

full_prompt: Given a 2022–2024 paper that introduced an optimization technique for coordinating electric vehicle charging at neighborhood scale, where the gist was to avoid transformer overloads while meeting driver needs, what follow-up question would examine how the method behaves under higher EV adoption and more uncertain user behavior?
chosen:
I first identify an anchor paper in the requested 2022–2024 window whose core contribution is neighborhood-scale EV charging coordination under transformer constraints and user-service requirements.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "2022 2023 neighborhood scale coordinated electric vehicle charging optimization transformer overload driver needs residential", "year_min": 2022, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returns several residential-distribution EV charging papers. The closest anchor is OpenAlex `W4389861216`, a 2023 paper on neighborhood-level coordinated EV charging using optimization to prevent distribution-transformer overload while satisfying EV departure-time and energy-demand constraints. Other nearby hits focus on pricing or feeder-level voltage control rather than transformer-constrained neighborhood scheduling.

I extract the structured genome of the anchor to make its mechanism, niche, and stated limitation explicit before designing an extension.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "W4389861216"}
```

[result]: The anchor’s mechanism is a model-predictive / receding-horizon optimization that schedules residential EV charging across a neighborhood subject to transformer capacity limits and user energy-deadline constraints. Its niche is low-voltage residential distribution with a shared service transformer. Its main observation is that coordinated charging can avoid overloads while still meeting most driver charging needs. Its limitation notes that evaluation used moderate EV penetration and relatively structured / known user inputs, leaving open performance under higher adoption and more uncertain arrival, departure, and energy-demand behavior.

I next search for adjacent work on uncertainty-aware and high-penetration EV charging to see whether the natural extension has already been addressed.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "high EV adoption uncertain user behavior stochastic robust coordinated charging residential transformer constraints", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search finds work on stochastic EV charging, robust optimization for distribution networks, and high-penetration EV adoption studies. OpenAlex `W4310396337` is a 2022 stochastic residential EV charging paper modeling uncertain arrivals and departures. OpenAlex `W4402147829` is a 2024 high-penetration distribution-transformer impact study, but it is more simulation-oriented than control-oriented.

I read the uncertainty-focused paper because it may already contain a mechanism that could extend the anchor.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W4310396337"}
```

[result]: This paper uses scenario-based stochastic optimization to account for uncertain EV arrival time, departure time, and initial state of charge. It shows that uncertainty-aware schedules reduce unmet charging demand and network violations compared with deterministic schedules. However, it does not specifically build on the anchor’s neighborhood transformer-overload controller, nor does it stress-test adoption levels across a single transformer population.

I also read the high-adoption impact paper to understand how penetration levels are usually parameterized and evaluated.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W4402147829"}
```

[result]: This study simulates increasing EV adoption rates in residential feeders and finds nonlinear growth in transformer overload hours once penetration passes moderate levels. It highlights that user behavior assumptions strongly affect overload risk, especially clustered evening arrivals. The paper is mainly diagnostic and does not propose a receding-horizon coordination method that jointly handles driver uncertainty and transformer constraints.

full_prompt: 我对基因编辑技术很感兴趣，但还不太清楚它在医学和农业中有哪些值得探索的问题。可以帮我推荐几个入门级的研究方向吗？

Investigate whether enhancing adult hippocampal neurogenesis improves memory in a mouse model of Alzheimer's disease. If behavioral gains are absent despite increased neurogenesis, pivot toward examining synaptic integration quality or network hyperexcitability instead of cell number.
chosen:
Initial rationale: I would start with a broad intervention study: increase adult hippocampal neurogenesis in an Alzheimer’s mouse model, quantify new neuron number with BrdU/DCX/NeuN, and test whether this is sufficient to rescue hippocampal memory. My working hypothesis is: “more adult-born dentate granule cells → better memory.”

```action
search query="Alzheimer mouse model enhanced adult hippocampal neurogenesis memory increased neurogenesis no behavioral rescue BDNF integration"
```

[result] A key study indicates that this broad “increase cell number” hypothesis is incomplete: in 5xFAD mice, increasing adult hippocampal neurogenesis alone was not sufficient to rescue memory. Exercise-like benefits required both increased neurogenesis and a pro-maturation/synaptic support signal such as BDNF. This suggests that new neuron number by itself may not drive behavioral recovery if the cells fail to mature, integrate, or stabilize hippocampal circuits.

Actually, this changes things: the central open question is not simply whether we can increase neurogenesis. That part is already partly solved. The contradiction is that enhanced neurogenesis can occur without memory rescue. I should narrow the project toward the quality and circuit consequences of new neurons: are adult-born neurons integrating functionally, forming appropriate synapses, and reducing rather than worsening AD-related network hyperexcitability?

```action
search query="adult-born granule cell synaptic integration Alzheimer's disease mouse hippocampal hyperexcitability neurogenesis"
```

[result] AD mouse models frequently show dentate/hippocampal circuit dysfunction, including altered excitatory-inhibitory balance, impaired synaptic plasticity, and network hyperexcitability. Adult-born neurons can either support pattern separation and memory if properly integrated, or fail to help if maturation, dendritic spine formation, inhibitory recruitment, or circuit timing is abnormal.

```json
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "revised_hypothesis": "In an Alzheimer's mouse model, enhancing adult hippocampal neurogenesis will improve memory only if adult-born neurons achieve proper synaptic maturation and normalize hippocampal circuit excitability; behavioral failure despite increased cell number will reflect poor integration or persistent hyperexcitability.",
    "model_and_groups": "Use 5xFAD mice and wild-type littermates. Compare control, neurogenesis-enhanced, and neurogenesis-enhanced plus integration-support groups, such as BDNF/TrkB pathway support or activity-guided enrichment. Include both sexes and age mice at an early symptomatic stage.",
    "primary_manipulation": "Increase adult hippocampal neurogenesis using a validated approach such as voluntary running, pharmacologic proneurogenic treatment, or inducible genetic expansion of adult neural progenitors. The key design is to separate increased new-neuron number from improved maturation/integration.",
    "behavioral_readouts": "Assess hippocampal-dependent memory with contextual fear conditioning, novel object location, Y-maze or radial-arm alternation, and pattern-separation tasks. If neurogenesis increases but these tasks do not improve, do not escalate cell-number manipulation; pivot to circuit-quality analyses.",
    "integration_and_circuit_assays": "Quantify DCX, BrdU/EdU-NeuN survival, dendritic complexity, spine density, immediate-early-gene recruitment during memory tasks, rabies or monosynaptic input mapping, and patch-clamp measures of excitatory and inhibitory synaptic input onto adult-born granule cells. Measure dentate/CA1 hyperexcitability using EEG, calcium imaging, c-Fos mapping, or ex vivo field recordings.",
    "decision_rule": "If increased neurogenesis correlates with memory rescue, test necessity by blocking maturation or silencing adult-born cells. If increased neurogenesis occurs without behavioral rescue, prioritize mechanisms of failed synaptic integration, abnormal inhibitory recruitment, or persistent hippocampal hyperexcitability rather than further increasing adult-born cell number."
  }
}
```

full_prompt: Draft a free-text research proposal for a randomized controlled trial comparing telehealth-based cognitive behavioral therapy with usual care for patients with chronic insomnia and comorbid depression.
chosen:
I’ll ground the trial proposal in current evidence on CBT-I, telehealth delivery, and depression comorbidity before proposing the RCT design.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"telehealth cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia chronic insomnia comorbid depression randomized controlled trial usual care outcomes ISI PHQ-9"}
```

[result]: Simulated search results suggest that CBT-I is guideline-recommended first-line therapy for chronic insomnia and can improve both insomnia severity and depressive symptoms. Digital or telehealth CBT-I has shown feasibility and efficacy, but more pragmatic randomized trials are needed in patients with clinically significant comorbid depression, especially comparing synchronous telehealth CBT-I against usual care.

```action
{"tool":"propose","idea_plan":{"Idea":"Conduct a pragmatic, parallel-group randomized controlled trial evaluating whether telehealth-based cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia improves sleep and depression outcomes compared with usual care among adults with chronic insomnia and comorbid depressive symptoms. The central hypothesis is that structured telehealth CBT-I will produce greater reductions in insomnia severity at 8 weeks and sustained improvements in depressive symptoms, sleep quality, fatigue, and functioning at 3 and 6 months compared with usual care.","ImplementationSteps":{"1":"Study design: A two-arm, assessor-blinded randomized controlled trial with 1:1 allocation to telehealth CBT-I or usual care. The study will enroll adults aged 18 years or older meeting DSM-5 criteria for chronic insomnia disorder and screening positive for at least moderate depressive symptoms, for example PHQ-9 score ≥10. Participants will be recruited from primary care, behavioral health, and sleep medicine clinics.","2":"Eligibility criteria: Inclusion criteria will include insomnia symptoms at least 3 nights per week for at least 3 months, Insomnia Severity Index score ≥15, access to a phone or video-capable device, and willingness to participate in telehealth visits. Exclusion criteria will include untreated obstructive sleep apnea or restless legs syndrome requiring primary treatment, bipolar disorder, psychosis, active substance use disorder requiring acute treatment, severe suicidal ideation requiring urgent intervention, night-shift work, pregnancy if sleep changes are the primary concern, or recent initiation/change of antidepressant or hypnotic medication within the past 4 weeks.","3":"Randomization and masking: Participants will be randomized using a computer-generated sequence stratified by baseline depression severity and current antidepressant use. Outcome assessors and data analysts will be blinded to treatment assignment. Participants and therapists cannot be blinded due to the behavioral nature of the intervention.","4":"Intervention: The experimental arm will receive manualized telehealth CBT-I delivered by trained therapists over 6 weekly sessions by secure video or telephone. Core components will include sleep education, sleep restriction therapy, stimulus control, cognitive restructuring, relaxation strategies, relapse prevention, and coordination with existing depression care when clinically indicated. Participants will complete sleep diaries electronically or by paper.","5":"Comparator: The usual care arm will continue treatment as directed by their clinicians, which may include antidepressant medication, sleep hygiene advice, pharmacotherapy for insomnia, or referral to behavioral health services. To preserve pragmatic relevance, usual care will not be restricted, but all sleep- and mood-related treatments will be tracked.","6":"Outcomes: The primary outcome will be change in Insomnia Severity Index score from baseline to 8 weeks. Key secondary outcomes will include depression severity measured by PHQ-9, sleep quality measured by Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index, fatigue, daytime functioning, anxiety symptoms, hypnotic medication use, treatment satisfaction, and remission rates for insomnia and depression. Outcomes will be assessed at baseline, post-treatment at 8 weeks, and follow-up at 3 and 6 months.","7":"Safety and risk management: Participants endorsing suicidal ideation on PHQ-9 item 9 or during study contacts will undergo protocolized risk assessment, clinician notification when appropriate, and referral to emergency or crisis services if needed. Adverse events, including worsening mood, excessive daytime sleepiness during sleep restriction, falls, or medication changes, will be monitored throughout the trial.","8":"Sample size and analysis: Assuming a clinically meaningful between-group difference of approximately 4 points on the Insomnia Severity Index, the trial will enroll an adequately powered sample allowing for attrition, for example approximately 200 participants total. Analyses will follow the intention-to-treat principle using mixed-effects regression models for repeated measures, adjusting for baseline values and stratification factors.
